narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, including your middle family income bracket and plans to attend full-time like most students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 97% chance that you would fail it, leading directly to the rejection of your application. This high failure probability means the school views you as a high-risk candidate unlikely to succeed on that critical exam right away. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your academic decile rankings from school years 1 and 3, both at the bottom of the 1st decile—far below the average 5th or 6th decile—pushing the prediction heavily toward failure by signaling consistently low performance relative to peers. Your LSAT score of 25, well under the average 37, was the second-biggest factor, further reinforcing the model's concern about your readiness for the rigors ahead. Even your strong undergrad GPA couldn't offset these, as your earlier school grades and test score carried the most weight in tipping the scales toward an expected bar exam struggle. Compared to typical students who pass, who often rank in higher deciles and score much better on the LSAT, your profile aligns more closely with those who face first-try failures. While factors like your full-time status matched the norm and didn't hurt you, they weren't enough to counterbalance the top concerns. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment focused on bar passage patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, strengthening your high school decile equivalents through additional coursework or certifications, and reapplying once you've addressed these key gaps—many students improve dramatically with targeted preparation. Schools value persistence, and refining these areas could shift your prediction favorably next time.
